category_title: Preschoolers at risk for developmental delays with mild language disorders
minimal_context: The student and a peer are playing with dramatic play toys and cars on a carpeted floor during a free play session.
dialogue:
TUTOR: It is time for free play. You and your partner can play with the dramatic play toys and cars on the carpet.

STUDENT: (Sits on the carpet, appearing lonely and making no comments to the peer.)

TUTOR: What are you going to play?

STUDENT: (Looks at the peer) Can I have the car?

TUTOR: Keep playing together.

STUDENT: (Does not engage in play with the peer) Can I have the car? Can I have the car? Can I have the car?

options.A: Implement a cognitive social learning model: 1) provide instruction of the use of specific social behaviors, 2) provide opportunity to rehearse the behaviors, 3) provide feedback and reinforcement on the use of the skills, and 4) provide opportunities for maintenance and generalization.
options.B: Spend the free play period working on projects and covertly listening to and watching the dyad, but never engaging in play directly. Intervene minimally, only for disputes or extreme raucousness during the session.
options.C: Empower the peer to exclude the student from the dramatic play area and cars if they are being too repetitive or withdrawn during the ten-minute free play session.
options.D: Before free play begins, show the student a brief two-minute video clip and discuss (i.e., priming them for success) how friends respectfully talk to one another, compliment one another, and ask to help each other.
